methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series D → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.5x) to $250M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Drones & Autonomous (found 0 same-stage, 0 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 4 same-sector peers exist in Drones & Autonomous, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

private.name: Zeitview
private.rationale: Same sector (Drones & Autonomous), same stage (Series D)

data.narrative:
Oxa is a Series D autonomous systems company founded by Paul Newman and Ingmar Posner, operating in the Drones & Autonomous sector. The company has raised $250M+ to date. The relevant peer universe consists of 5 private companies in the same sector (Joby Aviation, Nuro, Archer Aviation, Elroy Air, and Zeitview), with no direct public comparables available. The narrow peer set lacks sufficient same-stage or adjacent-stage companies to support a reliable peer-median valuation approach.

Given data constraints, stage-progression methodology is the primary applicable triangulation method. Using the $250M total raised as a valuation proxy and applying a 1.5x Series D step-up multiplier yields an implied valuation of $375M. This represents a disciplined extrapolation of capital deployment patterns in frontier-tech financing rounds, though it does not incorporate company-specific revenue, unit economics, or technology differentiation metrics.

The analysis supports a valuation range of $319M to $431M, with a median of $375M. This range reflects ±15% sensitivity around the stage-progression result. Key uncertainties include the absence of same-stage peer data to validate multiples, lack of visibility into Oxa's revenue or burn profile, and the nascent commercial maturity of autonomous systems markets. A peer-median approach remains inapplicable until additional Series D or late-stage comparable financing data emerges in the autonomous systems space.
